model_narrative: We plan preventive maintenance for the parts installed across the sections of a plant over a horizon of consecutive months. For each section-part pair, each event type, and each month we decide whether to perform that maintenance event, and for each month we decide whether to take a plant shutdown. Performing a maintenance event costs a per-event amount that depends on the part and the event type, and every shutdown incurs a fixed cost. The objective is to minimize the total cost over the whole plan.

sets.name: L
sets.doc: the plant sections

sets.name: I
sets.doc: the maintenance event types that can be performed on a part

sets.name: J
sets.doc: the part numbers

sets.name: T
sets.doc: the planning months, given as consecutive integers in chronological order; the months are exactly the integers from 1 up to the total number of months, so a month index plus an offset can be compared against the count of months to tell whether a window runs past the end of the horizon

sets.name: K
sets.doc: the valid section-part pairs; each member is a (section, part) pair stating that the part is installed in that section, and only these pairs are maintained

params.name: f
params.doc: the maximum allowed number of months between successive maintenance events for each section-part pair and event type, given as a count of months; an event of that type must occur within any run of consecutive months of this length

params.name: c
params.doc: the cost of performing one maintenance event for each section-part pair and event type, in INR

params.name: w
params.doc: the pullback window used to consolidate shutdowns, given as a count of months; it bounds how close together in time two shutdowns may occur

params.name: oc
params.doc: the cost incurred each time the plant is shut down, in INR

params.name: m
params.doc: a big-M constant that upper-bounds how many maintenance events can be performed simultaneously in a single month; large enough to never bind when a shutdown is active, lower case purely due to naming rules

vars.name: x
vars.doc: one when event type i is performed on section-part pair (l,j) in month t, zero otherwise

vars.name: o
vars.doc: one when the plant is shut down in month t, zero otherwise

vars.name: tc
vars.doc: the total cost over the whole plan, in INR

description: Each part in a section must receive each type of maintenance event often enough that it is never neglected for too long. For every section-part pair and every event type there is a maximum number of consecutive months that may pass without that event. Looking at any month as the start of a window of that length, at least one event of that type must be scheduled on that pair somewhere within the window. When such a window would extend past the end of the planning horizon, no requirement is imposed for that starting month.

For each section-part pair, each event type, and each month we decide whether to perform that maintenance event, and for each month we decide whether to take a plant shutdown. Performing a maintenance event costs a per-event amount that depends on the part and the event type, and every shutdown incurs a fixed cost. The objective is to minimize the total cost over the whole plan.

description: Maintenance can only happen in a month when the plant is shut down. For each month, if no shutdown is taken that month then no maintenance events of any type may be performed on any section-part pair, while if a shutdown is taken the number of events that month is allowed to be as large as needed. Express this by limiting the total number of maintenance events performed across all section-part pairs and event types in that month so that it can be positive only when the shutdown indicator for that month is on.

The objective is to minimize the total cost over the whole plan.

description: Shutdowns must be spread out in time and not bunched together. For any month taken as the start of a window whose length is the pullback window, at most one shutdown may occur within that window. When such a window would extend past the end of the planning horizon, no requirement is imposed for that starting month.

description: The total cost accounts for both maintenance and shutdowns. Add up, over every section-part pair, every event type, and every month, the per-event cost of each maintenance event that is performed, then add the fixed shutdown cost for every month in which a shutdown is taken. Set the total cost equal to this sum.

vars.doc: the total cost over the whole plan, in INR

description: To build the complete model, enforce the following relationships in order. First, each part in a section must receive each type of maintenance event often enough that it is never neglected for too long. For every section-part pair and every event type there is a maximum number of consecutive months that may pass without that event. Looking at any month as the start of a window of that length, at least one event of that type must be scheduled on that pair somewhere within the window. When such a window would extend past the end of the planning horizon, no requirement is imposed for that starting month. Second, maintenance can only happen in a month when the plant is shut down. For each month, if no shutdown is taken that month then no maintenance events of any type may be performed on any section-part pair, while if a shutdown is taken the number of events that month is allowed to be as large as needed. Express this by limiting the total number of maintenance events performed across all section-part pairs and event types in that month so that it can be positive only when the shutdown indicator for that month is on. Third, shutdowns must be spread out in time and not bunched together. For any month taken as the start of a window whose length is the pullback window, at most one shutdown may occur within that window. When such a window would extend past the end of the planning horizon, no requirement is imposed for that starting month. Finally, the total cost accounts for both maintenance and shutdowns. Add up, over every section-part pair, every event type, and every month, the per-event cost of each maintenance event that is performed, then add the fixed shutdown cost for every month in which a shutdown is taken. Set the total cost equal to this sum.
